critic_raw:
This ensemble design for readmission prediction has a fundamental structural flaw at its core comparison.

The ensemble combines XGBoost (trained on structured features) with a logistic regression model (trained on TF-IDF clinical note vectors), and compares against a logistic regression baseline trained on structured features only. This design simultaneously tests three different things: (1) whether adding clinical notes improves prediction, (2) whether a gradient-boosted model outperforms logistic regression on structured data, and (3) whether ensemble combination provides additional gain. Because the structured model in the ensemble is XGBoost, not logistic regression, the ensemble is not a fair incremental test of note inclusion — it is an XGBoost + logistic regression ensemble versus a plain logistic regression baseline. Any measured improvement could come entirely from replacing logistic regression with XGBoost on structured features, with notes contributing nothing.

The ensemble weights are 'tuned on validation set,' but the validation set is also used for hyperparameter tuning of both component models. This means the ensemble weights are selected based on the same data used to tune the components, creating a risk of overfitting the combination to validation-specific quirks. With 10,000 validation records and potentially several ensemble weight configurations tested, this is a real concern.

The TF-IDF vocabulary is limited to 500 features, which is quite restrictive for clinical notes that contain rich, specialized vocabulary. Key clinical terms related to readmission risk (e.g., specific medication names, diagnosis phrases, discharge condition descriptors) may fall outside the top-500 vocabulary after stopword removal and frequency filtering. The design does not validate that the TF-IDF representation retains clinically relevant terms or that note information is meaningfully captured at this vocabulary size.

The temporal leakage justification contains an error. The design states that 'a random split would allow a model trained on later data to see patterns from earlier clinical notes or treatment changes that occurred after the index admission, creating information leakage.' This is backwards — a random split would allow training on data from *any* time point, including future time points, which is the actual leakage concern. The stated mechanism (earlier clinical notes) is not the relevant leakage pathway, suggesting the design authors may not fully understand the temporal leakage issue they are addressing.
all_issues_raised: The ensemble replaces the structured model with XGBoost (not logistic regression as in the baseline), so any performance gain could be entirely attributable to the stronger structured model rather than clinical note inclusion; the design cannot distinguish these contributions without a same-architecture ablation.

Ensemble weights are tuned on the same validation set used for component model hyperparameter selection, creating a layered overfitting risk where the ensemble combination exploits validation-specific quirks that will not generalize to the test set.

The TF-IDF vocabulary is capped at 500 features, which may exclude clinically relevant low-to-mid-frequency terms critical for readmission prediction; the design does not validate that the vocabulary captures the signal it claims to represent.

The temporal leakage justification is internally inconsistent: the design claims random splits allow 'earlier clinical notes' to leak, which inverts the actual concern (random splits allow future information to enter training), suggesting a conceptual misunderstanding that may affect other design choices.
all_issues_adjudicated: The ensemble replaces the structured model with XGBoost (not logistic regression as in the baseline), so any performance gain could be entirely attributable to the stronger structured model rather than clinical note inclusion; the design cannot distinguish these contributions without a same-architecture ablation.

Ensemble weights are tuned on the same validation set used for component model hyperparameter selection, creating a layered overfitting risk where the ensemble combination exploits validation-specific quirks that will not generalize to the test set.

The TF-IDF vocabulary is capped at 500 features, which may exclude clinically relevant low-to-mid-frequency terms critical for readmission prediction; the design does not validate that the vocabulary captures the signal it claims to represent.

The temporal leakage justification is internally inconsistent: the design claims random splits allow 'earlier clinical notes' to leak, which inverts the actual concern (random splits allow future information to enter training), suggesting a conceptual misunderstanding that may affect other design choices.
